---
title: Setting up your own project
---

We will try to replicate the project you have just explored.

<!-- - Projections and why they matter.
In Victoria, map projections are mathematical tools used to flatten the Earth’s curved surface into a 2D map. Choosing the right one is critical because every projection introduces some level of distortion in distance, area, or shape. 
Using the wrong projection can lead to significant errors in measurement and data alignment—for example, a "gap" of roughly 1.8 metres exists between older GDA94 and modern GDA2020 datasets.
VicGrid2020 (EPSG: 7899): This is a "Lambert Conformal Conic" projection specifically designed for Victoria.
Why it matters: It is the most accurate choice for state-wide mapping. It minimises distortion across the entire state into a single zone, making it ideal for government planning and environmental analysis.
MGA2020 (Zones 54 and 55): Part of the "Map Grid of Australia," this uses a Universal Transverse Mercator (UTM) projection.
Why it matters: It is best for high-precision local work like surveying or construction. Victoria is split into two zones: Zone 54 (Western Vic) and Zone 55 (Melbourne and Eastern Vic). It keeps local measurements highly accurate but can cause "tears" if your project spans both zones.
Web Mercator (EPSG: 3857): The standard projection for Google Maps and other web platforms.
Why it matters: It is used for web-based visualisation. While convenient for browsing, it significantly distorts distance and area as you move away from the equator, so it should not be used for professional spatial analysis or surveying.
Data Alignment: If you mix data from the older GDA94 datum with the new GDA2020 datum without a proper transformation, your maps will be misaligned by about 1.5 to 1.8 metres. This could result in a fence line being placed in the middle of a road.
Accuracy in Measurement: In a projection like MGA2020, 1 metre on your screen is very close to 1 metre on the ground. In Web Mercator, that same "metre" could be significantly off depending on your latitude.
State-wide vs Local: VicGrid allows you to look at the whole state as one continuous map without "zone boundaries" causing issues, whereas MGA provides higher accuracy for a specific town or suburb.

- Where to get ready-made data from.
- Basemaps and linking XYZ Tiles / WMS Connections.
- Layer order, groups -->

## Set up folders for your project

QGIS projects save links to your data files, not the data itself. Organize your files like this:

- **Project Root**: Name it after your project (e.g., `GIS_Analysis_001/`).
- **Data folder**: Put all your spatial data here.
  - **Raw**: Original files.
  - **Processed**: Edited or cleaned data.
  - **Output**: Final maps or reports.
- **Scripts**: For any code or tools you use.

![Recommended Project Structure](../figs/GISI/Project_Setup/Recommended_FS.png)

## Start a new project

1. In QGIS, go to **Project > New**.
2. Or click the New Project icon in the toolbar.
3. Save it right away: **Project > Save**.

## Set project properties

Project properties control how your map works. Go to **Project > Properties**.

### Coordinate Reference System (CRS)

Choose the right CRS for your area. For Wurundjeri Country, use:

- GDA2020 (Geocentrict Datum of Australia 2020)/ MGA Zone 55 (EPSG:7855)
- Or VicGrid (EPSG:7899)

Older data might use GDA94 / VicGrid (EPSG:3111).

![Coordinate Reference System (CRS)](../figs/GISI/Project_Setup/Project_properties.png)

### General settings

- Set the project title and author.
- Choose background and selection colours.
- Set **Project Home** to your main folder so you can find your file paths.

![General Settings](../figs/GISI/Project_Setup/General.png)

### View settings

Define the **Project Full Extent**, think of it as a fence around your project, so you can zoom back to your main area quickly.

![View Settings Tab](../figs/GISI/Project_Setup/View_Settings.png)

### Data sources

Manage layers: if you like you can make some layers read-only or hide them from the legend.

![Data Sources Tab](../figs/GISI/Project_Setup/Data_S.png)

**Tips:**

Add favorites: In the **Browser Panel**, right-click a folder and select **Add as Favorite** for instant access.

Use relative paths: In **Project > Properties > General**, set **Save Paths** to "Relative" so you can move the project folder without breaking links.

## Add a basemap

Add a background map for context.

1. In the Browser Panel, right-click **XYZ Tiles** and choose **New Connection**.
2. Paste one of these URLs:

   - Google Satellite: `https://mt1.google.com/vt/lyrs=s&x={x}&y={y}&z={z}`
   - Google Maps: `https://mt1.google.com/vt/lyrs=m&x={x}&y={y}&z={z}`
   - Google Hybrid: `https://mt1.google.com/vt/lyrs=y&x={x}&y={y}&z={z}`
   - Google Terrain: `https://mt1.google.com/vt/lyrs=p&x={x}&y={y}&z={z}`

3. Add the layer to your project.

![Add a Basemap](../figs/GISI/Project_Setup/Add_XYZ.png)

## Add Victorian datasets

For local data:

1. In the Browser Panel, right-click **WMS/WMTS** and choose **New Connection**.
2. Paste this URL: `https://opendata.maps.vic.gov.au/geoserver/ows?service=WFS&request=getCapabilities`
3. Connect and add layers.

![Add a Victorian Data in QGIS](../figs/GISI/Project_Setup/Add_Local_Vic_Data.png)

<!-- TODO: Add instructions for opening a sample project -->
<!-- - How QGIS links into live documents (i.e. warning not to move source files around or information on how to update source locations if you do need to move things).

- Metadata and crediting.-->


**Feedback**
```{=html}
<iframe width="640px" height="480px" src="https://forms.office.com/Pages/ResponsePage.aspx?id=z_NbDvQft0aRdlLFOMIqTcB7gT7amOFBtQpSrGmDhYZUM0RKQUdGOVA5QTJKSVlXMjJWMlRXTFRKRyQlQCN0PWcu&embed=true" frameborder="0" marginwidth="0" marginheight="0" style="border: none; max-width:100%; max-height:100vh" allowfullscreen webkitallowfullscreen mozallowfullscreen msallowfullscreen> </iframe>
```
